Motion And Notice Of Motion To Quash Service Of Summons For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction
Off Calendar Sep-07-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Juliana Salfiti, Esq. of Leonidou & Rosin, Tel. 650.691.2888, for plaintiff and opposing counsel, by Court Call;
No other appearance.

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANT RPG ACOUSTAL SYSTEMS LLC and JEFFREY MADISON's Motion To Quash Service Of Summons For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction as follows: Defendants Jeffrey Madison and RPG Acoustical Systems LLC's motion to quash is granted as to Mr. Madison and denied as to RGPA. As to Mr. Madison, the evidence shows that he was merely acting as RPGA's agent and plaintiff Bayside Interiors, Inc. cites no individual conduct establishing the requisite minimum contacts by Mr. Madison. In Calder v. Jones (1984) 465 U.S. 783, the officer engaged in tortious conduct and could be personally liable. Bayside's allegations of alter ego liability-without any supporting evidence-does not support jurisdiction per Sonora Diamond Corp. v. Sup. Crt. (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 523, 540. As to RPGA, the declaration of Mike Tibbetts establishes specific personal jurisdiction. Mr. Tibetts' declaration shows that RPGA essentially assumed the Diffuser California contract and Mr. Madison, who had ongoing relations with Bayside, concedes that RPGA agreed to finish fabricating the acoustical panels and ship them to California. (Luberski, Inc. v. Oleficio F. LLI Amato S.R.L. (2009) 171 Cal. App. 4th 409.) Shisler v. Sanfer Sports Cars, Inc. (2006) 146 Cal.App.4th 1254 is distinguishable. Of particular importance in Shisler was the fact that, while the Florida car dealer operated a passive Web site that was available to residents in California, the dealer did not target California for its sales and there was no ongoing business relationship. Order signed. Judge: Harold E. Kahn; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Reporter: Nicole Rodich, CSR#11604, nrodich@sftc.org.
